identity.title: Other Guided Missile and Space Vehicle Parts and Auxiliary Equipment Manufacturing

lens.included_activities: Independent LMM manufacturers and prototype-to-production suppliers of guided-missile and space-vehicle parts, subassemblies, and auxiliary equipment other than propulsion, sold repeatedly to external primes, government programs, and commercial-space customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Complete vehicles, propulsion units, unrelated aircraft parts, captive divisions, pure distributors, government facilities, shells, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring development, qualified production-lot, spares, and sustainment revenue with engineering, configuration control, parts assurance, inspection, traceability, and mission-support obligations.

a.bridge: BLS data cover broader aerospace manufacturing rather than 336419 and show both engineering occupations and physical aerospace assembly. NASA parts-assurance sources establish dense quality, reliability, failure-mode, test, and supply-chain documentation. I map requirements review, engineering search, configuration, proposals, planning, quality-record analysis, parts screening, and test-data work as exposed; precision production, assembly, inspection execution, environmental test, and mission-assurance disposition are less substitutable. The broader proxy can overstate deployable exposure under security constraints.
a.rationale: High labor intensity and documentation-heavy engineering create meaningful exposure, but mission-critical physical production and verification remain central.
a.change_evidence: Cleared payroll-weighted task inventories across engineering, production, quality, test, program management, and security at the seven estimated suppliers would materially revise the range.
caveats: Classified and proprietary activity is underobserved.

rho.rationale: Secure assistance for search, documentation, parts screening, planning, proposals, and test-data analysis is plausible within five years. Export controls, classified networks, cyber accreditation, mission assurance, sparse failure data, validation, and customer approval constrain realization.
rho.change_evidence: Secure, audited pilots measuring labor savings, defects, escapes, and review time across non-safety-critical quality and engineering workflows would move the estimate.
caveats: Bounded judgment rather than observed adoption.
Final acceptance and mission assurance remain human-accountable.

e.rationale: Parts and auxiliary-equipment suppliers can have repeat qualified external programs, but the seven-firm universe likely includes strategic subsidiaries, captive facilities, project-only developers, or entities without transferable production. Clearances, funded backlog, qualification, and customer diversity are required.
e.change_evidence: Screen all candidates using CAGE, SAM.gov, DoD awards, NASA vendor data, ownership, facility clearances, quality approvals, program backlog, and audited EBITDA.
caveats: Auto-parts margin bridge is weak for space and missile suppliers.
Establishments may not correspond to transferable firms.

s5.bridge: Federal Reserve owner-age data cover small businesses generally, not missile or space parts transfers. I discount heavily for the tiny pool, strategic ownership, customer consent, clearances, export controls, contract novation, classified diligence, and program dependence. Supply-chain consolidation may raise strategic acquisitions but many are outside the LMM lens.
s5.rationale: Strategic demand exists, but national-security restrictions and an extremely small eligible universe make qualifying control transfers uncommon.
s5.change_evidence: CAGE ownership changes, contract novations, CFIUS disclosures, PitchBook/CapIQ transactions, and specialist adviser interviews since 2015 would produce a denominator-based rate.

q.rationale: Qualification, mission criticality, traceability, scarce capabilities, unique designs, and switching costs support high retention. Government cost analysis, cost-type contracts, prime cost-downs, learning curves, and follow-on competition share some savings.
q.change_evidence: Program contracts, contract type, certified cost data, fee histories, learning-curve clauses, qualification expense, and customer interviews would directly test retention.
caveats: No public contract sample measures pass-through.
Commercial fixed-price components differ from government development work.

d5.bridge: DoD FY2026 material provides $40 billion for Space Force, more than 30% above FY2025, and prioritizes missile defense and munitions; separate industrial-base documents identify missile guidance, antimissile defense, and space satellites as critical supply-chain end uses. These are funding signals, not five-year constant-price demand for eligible LMM 336419 firms. I translate them into strong but program-volatile growth, which can overstate output if funds stay in R&D or large primes.
d5.rationale: Missile replenishment, missile defense, proliferated satellite architectures, and commercial space support parts demand, while appropriations, program failure, rapid redesign, and prime insourcing create downside.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year funded procurement quantities, satellite constellation orders, commercial launch and spacecraft manifests, prime make-buy plans, and eligible-firm backlog would materially revise the range.

o.rationale: Retained demand requires accountable qualified operators to manufacture, inspect, test, trace, and deliver physical space and missile parts. Software and additive methods change production but do not remove mission-assurance responsibility.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of widespread prime or government insourcing, standardized commercial components eliminating specialist supply, or radically different architectures would move this estimate.
caveats: Operator share can migrate to primes.
This is separate from internal labor automation.

narrative.executive_view: Demand and labor intensity are strong, and qualified parts suppliers have high switching barriers. The obstacle is a seven-firm estimated universe constrained by security, customer approvals, and program concentration.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can assist requirements and parts-data search, configuration and quality documentation, proposal work, planning, procurement, failure-mode retrieval, and test-data review. Precision manufacture, assembly, environmental test, inspection execution, and acceptance remain physical and accountable.
narrative.value_capture: Qualification, traceability, mission criticality, scarce processes, and unique designs preserve savings. Government cost analysis, prime cost-downs, and contract learning curves share gains.
narrative.firm_availability: Only seven estimated candidates exist before filtering for independence, clearances, funded recurring programs, quality approvals, customer diversity, and transferable ownership.
narrative.demand_durability: Missile replenishment, missile defense, space architectures, and commercial missions support growth. Program cancellation, appropriations, prime insourcing, redesign, and qualification failures create severe target-level volatility.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Security and export controls, cyber accreditation, mission failure, counterfeit components, supply-chain quality, customer concentration, contract novation, and the weak margin bridge dominate.
narrative.principal_driver: Mission-critical qualified parts participate in expanding missile and space programs with strong switching barriers.
narrative.principal_weakness: The transferable acquisition pool is extremely small and exposed to single-program outcomes.

sources.title: Other Guided Missile and Space Vehicle Parts Manufacturing
sources.supports: Census identifies 336419 as other guided-missile and space-vehicle parts and auxiliary equipment manufacturing.

sources.title: DoD Background Briefing on FY 2026 Defense Budget
sources.supports: DoD states the budget prioritizes missile defense and munitions and provides $40 billion for Space Force, more than 30% above FY2025.

sources.title: NASA Electrical, Electronic, and Electromechanical Parts Assurance
sources.supports: NASA describes recommendations covering part performance, failure modes, tests, reliability, and supply-chain quality for spaceflight hardware.

sources.title: NASA Quality and Mission Assurance
sources.supports: NASA describes risk-based quality programs covering manufactured items, personnel, test equipment, facilities, processes, components, parts, and materials.

sources.title: FY2026 Defense Production Act Purchases Budget Justification
sources.supports: DoD identifies industrial shortfalls and critical supply-chain end uses including missile guidance, antimissile defense, and space-based satellites.

sources.title: 2024 Chartbook on Firms by Age of Primary Owner
sources.supports: Federal Reserve owner-age evidence used only as a broad succession proxy.
